steps.title: Overview: Layout Pipeline
steps.description: The variant tour introduced the data shapes. This tour follows the pipeline that fills them: defaults, normalization, geometry sizing, Corsi-specific layout, cut sheet creation, and diagnostic checks.

steps.title: Default Settings
steps.description: Defaults are real domain settings, not scattered UI fallbacks. They set the initial print design, filter dimensions, fan choice, fabrication settings, and preview toggles.

steps.title: Settings Normalization
steps.description: Normalization resolves ids into product objects and stores the normalized result. This gives later modules a consistent view of filter, fan, cutting, and print design choices.

steps.title: Raw Boundary Normalization
steps.description: The raw normalizer converts user-editable fields into canonical raw settings. It keeps URL compatibility while preventing old query parameters from leaking into the domain layer.

steps.title: Geometry Factory
steps.description: Geometry sizing is isolated in one factory. It turns normalized settings into working depth, chamber height, rim sizes, fan layers, and filter layer positions.

steps.title: Panel Generation
steps.description: Cut sheet creation delegates to panel generation first, then lays panels out into a drawing document. This means previews and export can share the same panel list.

steps.title: Corsi Layout Branch
steps.description: Corsi-Rosenthal mode has topology-specific defaults for fan count and face placement. The layout keeps those choices explicit instead of hiding them in the renderer.

steps.title: Layout Body
steps.description: The layout body combines raw normalization, normalized settings, geometry, cut sheet, SVG, diagnostics, and summaries. This is the single handoff used by UI, previews, and exports.

steps.title: Diagnostics
steps.description: Diagnostics inspect the finished layout and return user-facing warnings. They do not change geometry; they describe risk such as fan margins, unusual filter dimensions, or print bed fit.

steps.title: Quick Check
steps.text: Why do previews and exports use `LayoutResult` instead of reading DOM inputs directly?
answers: It centralizes normalization, derived geometry, cut panels, summary data, and diagnostics
It lets Three.js mutate form controls without event handlers
It avoids needing TypeScript types for the renderer
It stores all generated STL files in memory
steps.explanation: `LayoutResult` is assembled by `createLayout` on lines 1356-1378, and includes normalized settings, geometry output, cut sheet data, SVG, diagnostics, and summary data.

steps.title: Summary
steps.description: The layout pipeline turns boundary settings into a stable physical model. The next tour follows the laser geometry path that produces panel outlines, cut features, and SVG drawings from that model.
